calendar.matters:
Ntc Of Hearing For Petition For Relief From Goverment Claims
Off Calendar Nov-07-2019 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: Appearances:
Michael Beauchamp, 415-756-7331 Plaintiff in Pro Per.
Ryan Stevens, Deputy City Attorney, 415-554-3800 of the Office of the City Attorney for Defendant.

RULING - A R G U E D. The Court announces and explains the tentative ruling, the Court does not have jurisdiction. Plaintiff cites statute. (as fully reported in the reporter's notes/transcript) The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument rules as follows:

PETITIONER MICHAEL BEAUCHAMP's Petition For Relief From Goverment Claims' Requirement, Gov. Code 845.4.

The Court adopts the tentative ruling. Plaintiff Michael L. Beauchamp's petition for relief from government claims requirement is denied. Plaintiff seeks leave to file a late claim relating to an incident that allegedly occurred on October 11, 1990-more than 29 years ago. Plaintiff alleges that on June 18, 2019, he filed a claim relating to that incident which was denied, and that on June 26, 2019, he filed an application for leave to file a late claim, which was denied on June 28, 2019.

Government Code section 911.2 requires that a claim for personal injury must be presented "not later than six months after the accrual of the cause of action." When such a claim is not presented within the six-month period, the government entity may grant leave to present that claim, so long as it is presented "within a reasonable time not to exceed one year after the accrual of the cause of action." (Gov't Code 911.4.(b) (emphasis added).) If a claim is denied, the party may petition the court for an order relieving the party from the claim-filing requirement. (Gov't Code 946.6.) "When the underlying application to file a late claim is filed more than one year after the accrual of the cause of action, the court is without jurisdiction to grant relief under Government Code section 946.6." (JJ v. County of San Diego (2014) 223 Cal.App.4th 1214, 1221.) "The reason for the one-year statutory requirement is to 'protect[] a government entity from having to respond to a claim many years after the accrual of the action.'" (County of Los Angeles v. Superior Court (2001) 91 Cal.App.4th 1303, 1314.) Plaintiff's allegation that he suffers "ongoing damages" does not avoid the one-year statutory bar. (See Gov't Code 946.6(c).) Thus, because Plaintiff's application for leave to file a late claim was untimely, the court lacks jurisdiction to grant his petition.

Defendant's Counsel to submit a proposed order that is verbatim with the Court's ruling in compliance with CRC 3.1312.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman, Clerk: M. Goodman, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR#8600, maria.torreano@gmail.com, Reported. =(302/EPS)
